Diverse Data Collection: Ensure datasets include a balanced representation of women across age, race, and medical conditions, capturing gender-specific symptoms, outcomes, and experiences.    Bias Audits: Regularly audit AI models for gender bias by analyzing model performance across genders and correcting disparities in prediction accuracy and treatment recommendations.    Incorporate Gender-Specific Variables: Integrate gender-specific biological and social factors into the models, such as hormonal differences or pregnancy-related health concerns.    Collaboration with Experts: Engage interdisciplinary teams, including gender and healthcare specialists, to ensure AI models reflect women’s health needs and mitigate bias in design and implementation.    Transparency and Reporting: Mandate transparency in how models are developed, ensuring that gender representation is monitored and openly reported during model evaluation and deployment.    Continuous Feedback: Collect ongoing patient and clinician feedback to identify any gender-related biases that arise post-deployment and adjust models accordingly.
Be careful about representation.

Increase funding and selection of research projects looking at women’s health, mechanisms and impact of perimenopause on all health conditions which can begin as early as 35 years old ! Then you will have appropriate, accurate and sufficient data to address gender bias in AI

I think it would be interesting to erase gender and color data from AI models, I know long term it might not be sustainable or helpful, but instead of placing the gender and color at the end, possibly feeding the computer all the data about the condition and seeing what the AI model recommends for different people so the healthcare provider, might be able to see the options for treatment and take certain things more seriously. For example, if a woman comes in complaining about leg pain, the computer just receives the leg pain information, then the computer spits out if such and such is a white male we recommend (Y) if such and such is female we recommend (X) this would help healthcare providers see bias in AI models as well as hopefully improve standard of care for women and POC.

Ensuring that women are present in key discussions at all levels; partnering with our gender parity-focused employee resource group and external organizations (such as HBA) that similarly focus on that; ensuring that gender parity is part of discussions with any external vendors with whom we partner for work that involves use of AI models/tools.

AI is only as clever as the data we feed it with. We should be very careful to not just repeat/reproduce the currently existing gender healthgap in the AI space. I already saw some very mysogynious and excluding outcomes e.g. asking for a picture of a flight attendend. We tend to assume that written on a screen by AI it has to be true. We need to be super careful with this.
